Just now

--

Note: This article was originally written in Russian and translated into English with the assistance of AI.
In this article, I examine the qualitative and quantitative premise behind Transformers, first described in the paper “Attention Is All You Need” (2017). I will demonstrate that the core ideas underlying Transformers should not work — yet, remarkably, they do. How is this possible? Let’s investigate. From this, we will also understand what needs to change, and how, to bring AI closer to human-level capabilities.
The outline on which this article is based can be found via [this link].
Preliminary Notes on Mathematics in General — and Probability in Particular
For the past two centuries, scholars have sought to understand the foundations of mathematics. We will not enter into this debate here; instead, we will simply highlight the fundamental principles that, in our view, lie at the core of mathematics.

• Mathematics can be reduced to summation. In other words: absolutely any formula can be reduced to summation. This is a bold claim that only practical application can confirm or refute. Therefore, papanda implements a dedicated parser that enables such a reduction to summation.

• The fundamental contradiction driving the advancement of mathematics is that mathematical operations are introduced to simplify summation, yet they subsequently become more complex themselves due to the increasing complexity of the subject matter to which they are applied. One consequence of both summation as the essence of mathematics and this core contradiction is that mathematics fundamentally revolves around the transition from one number to another. In fact, it is only through this transition between numbers that mathematics can model change — which, in turn, serves as the very foundation of all machine learning and AI.
These foundations of mathematics reveal its limitations. In the real world that mathematics describes, everything moves through contradictions. In fact, this world is the very essence of development and movement. However, development is not identical to change. Mathematics is good at describing change, but falls short with development. At the same time, development inherently includes change, which is why mathematics manages to “hit the mark” in its description of reality.
Probability serves as the foundation of AI and represents one of the fundamental mathematical disciplines. However, classical probability suffers from four limitations that hinder its ability to adequately describe the real world:

• Probability does not address motion, whereas motion is the fundamental essence of this world;

• Probability seeks to evaluate an outcome from an initial point without accounting for the execution itself — the transition from the starting point to the result. We evaluate the ratio a/b or c/b before the “selection” occurs that produces either a or c. If the world changes in the interim between the balls being in the bag and a ball being selected, probability fails to register this;

• In probability, a and c are treated as strictly isolated from one another, whereas in dialectics, no such rigid separation exists;

• It operates only after opposites have already been identified — for example, once we have established the “bag of balls” versus “selected blue ball” relationship.
Probability works for the same reason that mathematics works. Probability describes change, and change is a part of development. In simple cases, change can stand in for development, yet change does not provide understanding. You can tell three stones apart from two, but you cannot understand through this alone why three stones became two.
Transformers: An Applied Manifestation of the Fundamental Limitations of Mathematics
In the 20th century, computers and algorithms emerged, paving the way for automation. In the mid-20th century, the task of translating foreign texts arose. Later, this task expanded to text “generation.”
Once you need to generate text, especially on a computer, you must define the very essence of text generation. Is it mere keyboard typing? A meaningful sequence? A set of words? Initially, the assumption was that a sentence could well be defined as a sequence of words. This seems logical: after all, we write a sentence word by word. However, this approach overlooks the fact that “writing” or “speaking” a sentence is far from the same as “creating” it. We create sentences “in our heads,” moving from the predicate and the subject, rather than constructing the sentence word by word…
Nevertheless, the word-by-word approach to sentence construction prevailed — and recurrent neural networks (RNNs) emerged. In recurrent networks, words are generated one by one. But how? How is it possible to guess which word will follow the current one? Let’s look at this task again, but a bit more abstractly: for some A, we need to determine another entity, B. We are looking for a distinction, a difference, a change. We first look for change in general, and then for a specific change. Or to put it another way: we take the number 3 and want to obtain the number 15 from it, for which we find the number 4 to multiply by. We get 12 instead of 15, but you must agree, this is close — an error of only 3. We can continue this fitting process. In fact, this is all recurrent networks do. Training such networks is simply finding the number as close to 5 as possible.
However, recurrent networks did not always succeed — generating long texts and translations were their weak points. This is where the network stumbled. Then came the idea to augment the network with an attention mechanism.
Since the network stumbled precisely because it lost context while generating word by word, a way to retain this context had to be devised. The solution: for the word of interest, we must determine the importance of all other words in the sentence. That is, the model had to be trained not only to see which word follows which, but also to recognize which word in the sentence — regardless of its position — influences the appearance of the word of interest. This was already a significant step forward in treating a sentence as more than just an ordinary set of words. Now, they took the number 3, aimed for 15, found the number 4, but multiplied it by 1.2 to reflect its importance. This gave 4.8, which resulted in 14.5, which is quite close to 15 — certainly closer than a standard network.
Notice that you simply need to multiply 4 by 1.2; it does not matter at all what it represents. Not at all? Well, almost. For the 1.2 itself, it doesn’t matter what it reflects — length, quantity, etc. However, when you are given a set of such numbers from which 1.2 emerged, you can evaluate the change, and change is already a qualitative process. We will not pursue this topic further here, but it was necessary to note it.
Thus, recurrent networks were supplemented with an attention mechanism. But look: you multiply 4 by 1.2. Is there any way to discard the 4 and find 4.8 directly? Yes, you just need to “amplify” that very 1.2 — that is, tweak everything in the process where we obtained it so that we get 4.8 instead of 1.2. This seems identical: multiplying 4 by 1.2 versus obtaining the value 4.8 from 1.2. If you consider this statically, it truly will be identical for you. But it is different when viewed dynamically.
This is the exact challenge where Transformers emerge. The essence of Transformers is to completely frame sentence generation as the generation of words linked by meaning rather than by sequence. However, even in this task, Transformers do not step outside the boundaries of the fundamental mathematical principles we described above.
And where does probability fit into this? Probability enters at the moment when the “raw” word properties, obtained through the application of weight matrices, need to be evaluated for their “significance.” Softmax is used for this purpose. As a result, a probability is obtained, and based on it, the next word is estimated. This is how a sentence is generated.
Let us summarize. Transformers and modern AI: 1) convert text into numbers; 2) attempt to model changes in the world through changes in numbers; 3) try to guess the formula (the “weights”) for such numeric changes; 4) apply probability to perform this guessing.
How to Overcome the Fundamental Limitation of Transformers
We have seen that Transformers do not operate outside the foundational principles of mathematics: summation, derivative operations, and the transition from one number to another. Is this actually a problem? Yes, it is. And it stems directly from the inherent limitations of mathematics — mathematics is fundamentally incapable of describing a contradictory, dialectical world. Yet, this is precisely the nature of the reality that surrounds us. Of course, one can fit our world into the constraints of mathematical capabilities. Why is this possible? Because development, which is the very essence of dialectics, inherently encompasses change, which is the essence of mathematics. Therefore, mathematics can be used to “explain” and “predict” this world.
However, such an explanation comes at a high cost. First, the results are fundamentally probabilistic. Second, vast computational resources must be expended on training and subsequently deploying these models.
This means a different approach is required. How should it differ? It must align as closely as possible with development — with a dialectical conception of the world. This implies that merely evaluating change is insufficient. Furthermore, probability cannot serve as the decisive element of such an approach. Most importantly, we must seek out opposites. Not probabilities — opposites. Why? Because everything is development. Development itself is understood as the transition from a rudimentary process, through its evolution into a contradiction, to the resolution of that contradiction. A contradiction emerges precisely from opposites. Therefore, identifying opposites is the foundational basis for finding contradictions, and consequently, for describing the motion and development of anything in the world. Since the human brain also comprehends the world dialectically — that is, through motion — the search for opposites represents a genuine simulation of actual cognitive activity.
What Must Be Done to Implement an Alternative Approach?
Ideally, what we need is a mathematics that approaches dialectics as closely as possible — that is, a form of mathematics capable of working with contradictions, or more precisely, one that functions directly on contradictions. I attempted to demonstrate the fundamental feasibility of such a mathematics in the article Making AI Work with Contradictions: The Hash Mathematics Concept.
Yet, since such a mathematics does not yet exist, what is to be done? We must attempt to find an approach that differs from a purely probabilistic understanding of the world. This approach should not entirely dismiss probabilistic comprehension, as it is self-evident that it performs reasonably well. However, this framework must go beyond the mere calculation of probabilities…
What could this be? Infinity. This is not a joke. Every process in the world is potentially infinite and emerged as a “node” within an endless process of development. Each such node can be unfolded into the underlying processes that define it. These processes, considered at infinity, must be identified in such a way that they lead precisely to this specific node. We understand that this sounds overly abstract. In the near future, we plan to implement this algorithm and will dedicate a separate article to it. However, it is worth noting right now that this abstractness is merely illusory.
